From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Study of collection and analysis of data

This article is about the study of data. For a value derived from a sample, see [Statistic](/wiki/Statistic "Statistic"). For other uses, see [Statistics (disambiguation)](/wiki/Statistics_(disambiguation) "Statistics (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Standard_Normal_Distribution-en.svg/330px-Standard_Normal_Distribution-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Standard_Normal_Distribution-en.svg)

The [normal distribution](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"), a very common [probability density](/wiki/Probability_density_function "Probability density function"), is used extensively in [inferential statistics](/wiki/Inferential_statistics "Inferential statistics").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Iris_Pairs_Plot.svg/330px-Iris_Pairs_Plot.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Iris_Pairs_Plot.svg)

[Scatter plots](/wiki/Scatter_plot "Scatter plot") and [line charts](/wiki/Line_chart "Line chart") are used in [descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics") to show the observed relationships between different variables, here using the [*Iris* flower data set](/wiki/Iris_flower_data_set "Iris flower data set").

**Statistics** (from [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *[Statistik](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Statistik#German "wikt:Statistik")*, orig. "description of a [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), a country"[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)) is the discipline that concerns the collection, organization, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of [data](/wiki/Data "Data").[[2]](#cite_note-def-2) In applying statistics to a scientific, industrial, or social problem, it is conventional to begin with a [statistical population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population") or a [statistical model](/wiki/Statistical_model "Statistical model") to be studied. Populations can be diverse groups of people or objects such as "all people living in a country" or "every atom composing a crystal". Statistics deals with every aspect of data, including the planning of data collection in terms of the design of [surveys](/wiki/Statistical_survey "Statistical survey") and [experiments](/wiki/Experimental_design "Experimental design").[[3]](#cite_note-Dodge-3) Statistics is deeply related to subjects like physics, chemistry, geography, geopolitics, and especially mathematics.

When [census](/wiki/Census "Census") data (comprising every member of the target population) cannot be collected, [statisticians](/wiki/Statistician "Statistician") collect data by developing specific experiment designs and survey [samples](/wiki/Sample_(statistics) "Sample (statistics)"). Representative sampling assures that inferences and conclusions can reasonably extend from the sample to the population as a whole. An [experimental study](/wiki/Experimental_study "Experimental study") involves taking measurements of the system under study, manipulating the system, and then taking additional measurements using the same procedure to determine if the manipulation has modified the values of the measurements. In contrast, an [observational study](/wiki/Observational_study "Observational study") does not involve experimental manipulation.

Two main statistical methods are used in [data analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis"): [descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics"), which summarize data from a sample using [indexes](/wiki/Index_(statistics) "Index (statistics)") such as the [mean](/wiki/Mean "Mean") or [standard deviation](/wiki/Standard_deviation "Standard deviation"), and [inferential statistics](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference"), which draw conclusions from data that are subject to random variation (e.g., observational errors, sampling variation).[[4]](#cite_note-LundResearchLtd-4) Descriptive statistics are most often concerned with two sets of properties of a *distribution* (sample or population): *[central tendency](/wiki/Central_tendency "Central tendency")* (or *location*) seeks to characterize the distribution's central or typical value, while *[dispersion](/wiki/Statistical_dispersion "Statistical dispersion")* (or *variability*) characterizes the extent to which members of the distribution depart from its center and each other. Inferences made using [mathematical statistics](/wiki/Mathematical_statistics "Mathematical statistics") employ the framework of [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), which deals with the analysis of random phenomena.

A standard statistical procedure involves the collection of data leading to a [test of the relationship](/wiki/Statistical_hypothesis_testing "Statistical hypothesis testing") between two statistical data sets, or a data set and synthetic data drawn from an idealized model. A hypothesis is proposed for the statistical relationship between the two data sets, an [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis "Alternative hypothesis") to an idealized [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis") of no relationship between two data sets. Rejecting or disproving the null hypothesis is done using statistical tests that quantify the sense in which the null can be proven false, given the data that are used in the test. Working from a null hypothesis, two basic forms of error are recognized: [Type I errors](/wiki/Type_I_error "Type I error") (null hypothesis is rejected when it is in fact true, giving a "false positive") and [Type II errors](/wiki/Type_II_error "Type II error") (null hypothesis fails to be rejected when it is in fact false, giving a "false negative"). Multiple problems have come to be associated with this framework, ranging from obtaining a sufficient sample size to specifying an adequate null hypothesis.[[4]](#cite_note-LundResearchLtd-4)

Statistical measurement processes are also prone to error with regard to the data they generate. Many of these errors are classified as random (noise) or systematic ([bias](/wiki/Bias_(statistics) "Bias (statistics)")), but other types of errors (e.g., blunder, such as when an analyst reports incorrect units) can also occur. The presence of [missing data](/wiki/Missing_data "Missing data") or [censoring](/wiki/Censoring_(statistics) "Censoring (statistics)") may result in biased estimates, and specific techniques have been developed to address these problems.

## Introduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction")]

Main article: [Outline of statistics](/wiki/Outline_of_statistics "Outline of statistics")

> "Statistics is both the science of uncertainty and the technology of extracting information from data." - featured in the International Encyclopedia of Statistical Science.[[5]](#cite_note-Hand20102-5)

Statistics is the discipline that deals with [data](/wiki/Data "Data"), facts and figures with which meaningful information is inferred. Data may represent a numerical value, in form of quantitative data, or a label, as with qualitative data. Data may be collected, presented and summarised, in one of two methods called descriptive statistics. Two elementary summaries of data, singularly called a statistic, are the mean and dispersion. Whereas inferential statistics interprets data from a population sample to induce statements and predictions about a population.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[5]](#cite_note-Hand20102-5)

Statistics is regarded as a body of science[[8]](#cite_note-Moses-8) or a branch of mathematics.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is based on probability, a branch of mathematics that studies random events. Statistics is considered the science of uncertainty. This arises from the ways to cope with measurement and sampling error as well as dealing with uncertanties in modelling. Although probability and statistics were once paired together as a single subject, they are conceptually distinct from one another. The former is based on deducing answers to specific situations from a general theory of probability, meanwhile statistics induces statements about a population based on a data set. Statistics serves to bridge the gap between probability and applied mathematical fields.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[5]](#cite_note-Hand20102-5)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Some consider statistics to be a distinct [mathematical science](/wiki/Mathematical_sciences "Mathematical sciences") rather than a branch of mathematics. While many scientific investigations make use of data, statistics is generally concerned with the use of data in the context of uncertainty and decision-making in the face of uncertainty.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Statistics is indexed at 62, a subclass of probability theory and stochastic processes, in the Mathematics Subject Classification.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Mathematical statistics is covered in the range 276-280 of subclass QA (science > mathematics) in the Library of Congress Classification.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The word statistics ultimately comes from the Latin word Status, meaning "situation" or "condition" in society, which in late Latin adopted the meaning "state". Derived from this, political scientist Gottfried Achenwall, coined the German word statistik (a summary of how things stand). In 1770, the term entered the English language through German and referred to the study of political arrangements. The term gained its modern meaning in the 1790s in John Sinclair's works.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In modern German, the term statistik is synonymous with mathematical statistics. The term statistic, in singular form, is used to describe a function that returns its value of the same name.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Statistical data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Statistical data")]

### Data collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Data collection")]

#### Sampling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sampling")]

When full census data cannot be collected, statisticians collect sample data by developing specific [experiment designs](/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments") and [survey samples](/wiki/Survey_sampling "Survey sampling"). Statistics itself also provides tools for prediction and forecasting through [statistical models](/wiki/Statistical_model "Statistical model").

To use a sample as a guide to an entire population, it is important that it truly represents the overall population. Representative [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") ensures that inferences and conclusions can safely be extended from the sample to the population as a whole. A major problem lies in determining the extent to which the chosen sample is actually representative. Statistics offers methods to estimate and correct for any bias in the sample and data collection procedures. There are also methods of experimental design that can lessen these issues at the outset of a study, strengthening its ability to discern truths about the population.

Sampling theory is part of the [mathematical discipline](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") of [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"). Probability is used in [mathematical statistics](/wiki/Statistical_theory "Statistical theory") to study the [sampling distributions](/wiki/Sampling_distribution "Sampling distribution") of [sample statistics](/wiki/Sample_statistic "Sample statistic") and, more generally, the properties of [statistical procedures](/wiki/Statistical_decision_theory "Statistical decision theory"). The use of any statistical method is valid only when the system or population under consideration satisfies the assumptions of the method. The difference in point of view between classic probability theory and sampling theory is, roughly, that probability theory starts with the given parameters of a total population to [deduce](/wiki/Deductive_reasoning "Deductive reasoning") probabilities that pertain to samples. Statistical inference, however, moves in the opposite direction—[inductively inferring](/wiki/Inductive_reasoning "Inductive reasoning") from samples to the parameters of a larger or total population.

#### Experimental and observational studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Experimental and observational studies")]

A common goal for a statistical research project is to investigate [causality](/wiki/Causality "Causality"), and in particular to draw a conclusion on the effect of changes in the values of predictors or [independent variables on dependent variables](/wiki/Dependent_and_independent_variables "Dependent and independent variables"). There are two major types of causal statistical studies: [experimental studies](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment") and [observational studies](/wiki/Observational_study "Observational study"). In both types of studies, the effect of differences of an independent variable (or variables) on the behavior of the dependent variable are observed. The difference between the two types lies in how the study is actually conducted. Each can be very effective. An experimental study involves taking measurements of the system under study, manipulating the system, and then taking additional [measurements with different levels](/wiki/Level_of_measurement "Level of measurement") using the same procedure to determine if the manipulation has modified the values of the measurements. In contrast, an observational study does not involve [experimental manipulation](/wiki/Scientific_control "Scientific control"). Instead, data are gathered and correlations between predictors and response are investigated. While the tools of data analysis work best on data from [randomized studies](/wiki/Randomized_controlled_trial "Randomized controlled trial"), they are also applied to other kinds of data—like [natural experiments](/wiki/Natural_experiment "Natural experiment") and [observational studies](/wiki/Observational_study "Observational study")[[19]](#cite_note-19)—for which a statistician would use a modified, more structured estimation method (e.g., [difference in differences estimation](/wiki/Difference_in_differences "Difference in differences") and [instrumental variables](/wiki/Instrumental_variable "Instrumental variable"), among many others) that produce [consistent estimators](/wiki/Consistent_estimator "Consistent estimator").

##### Experiments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Experiments")]

The basic steps of a statistical experiment are:

1. Planning the research, including finding the number of replicates of the study, using the following information: preliminary estimates regarding the size of [treatment effects](/wiki/Average_treatment_effect "Average treatment effect"), [alternative hypotheses](/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis "Alternative hypothesis"), and the estimated [experimental variability](/wiki/Experimental_error "Experimental error"). Consideration of the selection of experimental subjects and the ethics of research is necessary. Statisticians recommend that experiments compare (at least) one new treatment with a standard treatment or control, to allow an unbiased estimate of the difference in treatment effects.
2. [Design of experiments](/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments"), using [blocking](/wiki/Blocking_(statistics) "Blocking (statistics)") to reduce the influence of [confounding variables](/wiki/Confounding_variable "Confounding variable"), and [randomized assignment](/wiki/Randomized_assignment "Randomized assignment") of treatments to subjects to allow [unbiased estimates](/wiki/Bias_of_an_estimator "Bias of an estimator") of treatment effects and experimental error. At this stage, the experimenters and statisticians write the *[experimental protocol](/wiki/Protocol_(natural_sciences) "Protocol (natural sciences)")* that will guide the performance of the experiment and which specifies the *primary analysis* of the experimental data.
3. Performing the experiment following the [experimental protocol](/wiki/Protocol_(natural_sciences) "Protocol (natural sciences)") and [analyzing the data](/wiki/Analysis_of_variance "Analysis of variance") following the experimental protocol.
4. Further examining the data set in secondary analyses, to suggest new hypotheses for future study.
5. Documenting and presenting the results of the study.

Experiments on human behavior have special concerns. The famous [Hawthorne study](/wiki/Hawthorne_study "Hawthorne study") examined changes to the working environment at the Hawthorne plant of the [Western Electric Company](/wiki/Western_Electric_Company "Western Electric Company"). The researchers were interested in determining whether increased illumination would increase the productivity of the [assembly line](/wiki/Assembly_line "Assembly line") workers. The researchers first measured the productivity in the plant, then modified the illumination in an area of the plant and checked if the changes in illumination affected productivity. It turned out that productivity indeed improved (under the experimental conditions). However, the study is heavily criticized today for errors in experimental procedures, specifically for the lack of a [control group](/wiki/Control_group "Control group") and [blindness](/wiki/Double-blind "Double-blind"). The [Hawthorne effect](/wiki/Hawthorne_effect "Hawthorne effect") refers to finding that an outcome (in this case, worker productivity) changed due to observation itself. Those in the Hawthorne study became more productive not because the lighting was changed but because they were being observed.[[20]](#cite_note-pmid17608932-20)

##### Observational study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Observational study")]

An example of an observational study is one that explores the association between smoking and lung cancer. This type of study typically uses a survey to collect observations about the area of interest and then performs statistical analysis. In this case, the researchers would collect observations of both smokers and non-smokers, perhaps through a [cohort study](/wiki/Cohort_study "Cohort study"), and then look for the number of cases of lung cancer in each group.[[21]](#cite_note-21) A [case-control study](/wiki/Case-control_study "Case-control study") is another type of observational study in which people with and without the outcome of interest (e.g. lung cancer) are invited to participate and their exposure histories are collected.

### Types of data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Types of data")]

Main articles: [Statistical data type](/wiki/Statistical_data_type "Statistical data type") and [Levels of measurement](/wiki/Levels_of_measurement "Levels of measurement")

Various attempts have been made to produce a taxonomy of [levels of measurement](/wiki/Level_of_measurement "Level of measurement"). The psychophysicist [Stanley Smith Stevens](/wiki/Stanley_Smith_Stevens "Stanley Smith Stevens") defined nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales. Nominal measurements do not have meaningful rank order among values, and permit any one-to-one (injective) transformation. Ordinal measurements have imprecise differences between consecutive values, but have a meaningful order to those values, and permit any order-preserving transformation. Interval measurements have meaningful distances between measurements defined, but the zero value is arbitrary (as in the case with [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") and [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") measurements in [Celsius](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") or [Fahrenheit](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")), and permit any linear transformation. Ratio measurements have both a meaningful zero value and the distances between different measurements defined, and permit any rescaling transformation.

Because variables conforming only to nominal or ordinal measurements cannot be reasonably measured numerically, sometimes they are grouped together as [categorical variables](/wiki/Categorical_variable "Categorical variable"), whereas ratio and interval measurements are grouped together as [quantitative variables](/wiki/Variable_(mathematics)#Applied_statistics "Variable (mathematics)"), which can be either [discrete](/wiki/Probability_distribution#Discrete_probability_distribution "Probability distribution") or [continuous](/wiki/Probability_distribution#Continuous_probability_distribution "Probability distribution"), due to their numerical nature. Such distinctions can often be loosely correlated with [data type](/wiki/Data_type "Data type") in computer science, in that dichotomous categorical variables may be represented with the [Boolean data type](/wiki/Boolean_data_type "Boolean data type"), polytomous categorical variables with arbitrarily assigned [integers](/wiki/Integer "Integer") in the [integral data type](/wiki/Integer_(computer_science) "Integer (computer science)"), and continuous variables with the [real data type](/wiki/Real_data_type "Real data type") involving [floating-point arithmetic](/wiki/Floating-point_arithmetic "Floating-point arithmetic"). But the mapping of computer science data types to statistical data types depends on which categorization of the latter is being implemented.

Other categorizations have been proposed. For example, Mosteller and Tukey (1977)[[22]](#cite_note-22) distinguished grades, ranks, counted fractions, counts, amounts, and balances. Nelder (1990)[[23]](#cite_note-23) described continuous counts, continuous ratios, count ratios, and categorical modes of data. (See also: Chrisman (1998),[[24]](#cite_note-24) van den Berg (1991).[[25]](#cite_note-25))

The issue of whether it is appropriate to apply different kinds of statistical methods to data obtained from different kinds of measurement procedures is complicated by issues concerning the transformation of variables and the precise interpretation of research questions. "The relationship between the data and what they describe merely reflects the fact that certain kinds of statistical statements may have truth values that are not invariant under some transformations. Whether a transformation is sensible to contemplate depends on the question one is trying to answer."[[26]](#cite_note-26): 82

### Descriptive statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Descriptive statistics")]

Main article: [Descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics")

A **descriptive statistic** (in the [count noun](/wiki/Count_noun "Count noun") sense) is a [summary statistic](/wiki/Summary_statistic "Summary statistic") that quantitatively describes or summarizes features of a collection of [information](/wiki/Information "Information"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) while **descriptive statistics** in the [mass noun](/wiki/Mass_noun "Mass noun") sense is the process of using and analyzing those statistics. Descriptive statistics is distinguished from [inferential statistics](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference") (or inductive statistics), in that descriptive statistics aims to summarize a [sample](/wiki/Sample_(statistics) "Sample (statistics)"), rather than use the data to learn about the [population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population") that the sample of data is thought to represent.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Inferential statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Inferential statistics")]

Main article: [Statistical inference](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference")

**Statistical inference** is the process of using [data analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis") to deduce properties of an underlying [probability distribution](/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution").[[29]](#cite_note-Oxford-29) Inferential statistical analysis infers properties of a [population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population"), for example by testing hypotheses and deriving estimates. It is assumed that the observed data set is [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") from a larger population. Inferential statistics can be contrasted with [descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics"). Descriptive statistics is solely concerned with properties of the observed data, and it does not rest on the assumption that the data come from a larger population.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Terminology and theory of inferential statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics")]

##### Statistics, estimators and pivotal quantities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Statistics, estimators and pivotal quantities")]

Consider [independent identically distributed (IID) random variables](/wiki/Independent_identically_distributed "Independent identically distributed") with a given [probability distribution](/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution"): standard [statistical inference](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference") and [estimation theory](/wiki/Estimation_theory "Estimation theory") defines a [random sample](/wiki/Random_sample "Random sample") as the [random vector](/wiki/Random_vector "Random vector") given by the [column vector](/wiki/Column_vector "Column vector") of these IID variables.[[31]](#cite_note-Piazza-31) The [population](/wiki/Statistical_population "Statistical population") being examined is described by a probability distribution that may have unknown parameters.

A statistic is a random variable that is a function of the random sample, but *not a function of unknown parameters*. The probability distribution of the statistic, though, may have unknown parameters. Consider now a function of the unknown parameter: an [estimator](/wiki/Estimator "Estimator") is a statistic used to estimate such function. Commonly used estimators include [sample mean](/wiki/Sample_mean "Sample mean"), unbiased [sample variance](/wiki/Sample_variance "Sample variance") and [sample covariance](/wiki/Sample_covariance "Sample covariance").

A random variable that is a function of the random sample and of the unknown parameter, but whose probability distribution *does not depend on the unknown parameter* is called a [pivotal quantity](/wiki/Pivotal_quantity "Pivotal quantity") or pivot. Widely used pivots include the [z-score](/wiki/Z-score "Z-score"), the [chi square statistic](/wiki/Chi-squared_distribution#Applications "Chi-squared distribution") and Student's [t-value](/wiki/Student%27s_t-distribution#How_the_t-distribution_arises "Student's t-distribution").

Between two estimators of a given parameter, the one with lower [mean squared error](/wiki/Mean_squared_error "Mean squared error") is said to be more [efficient](/wiki/Efficient_estimator "Efficient estimator"). Furthermore, an estimator is said to be [unbiased](/wiki/Unbiased_estimator "Unbiased estimator") if its [expected value](/wiki/Expected_value "Expected value") is equal to the [true value](/wiki/True_value "True value") of the unknown parameter being estimated, and asymptotically unbiased if its expected value converges at the [limit](/wiki/Limit_(mathematics) "Limit (mathematics)") to the true value of such parameter.

Other desirable properties for estimators include: [UMVUE](/wiki/UMVUE "UMVUE") estimators that have the lowest variance for all possible values of the parameter to be estimated (this is usually an easier property to verify than efficiency) and [consistent estimators](/wiki/Consistent_estimator "Consistent estimator") which [converges in probability](/wiki/Converges_in_probability "Converges in probability") to the true value of such parameter.

This still leaves the question of how to obtain estimators in a given situation and carry the computation, several methods have been proposed: the [method of moments](/wiki/Method_of_moments_(statistics) "Method of moments (statistics)"), the [maximum likelihood](/wiki/Maximum_likelihood "Maximum likelihood") method, the [least squares](/wiki/Least_squares "Least squares") method and the more recent method of [estimating equations](/wiki/Estimating_equations "Estimating equations").

##### Null hypothesis and alternative hypothesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Null hypothesis and alternative hypothesis")]

Interpretation of statistical information can often involve the development of a [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis") which is usually (but not necessarily) that no relationship exists among variables or that no change occurred over time.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) The [alternative hypothesis](/wiki/Alternative_hypothesis "Alternative hypothesis") is the name of the hypothesis that contradicts the null hypothesis.

The best illustration for a novice is the predicament encountered by a [criminal trial](/wiki/Criminal_trial "Criminal trial"). The null hypothesis, H0, asserts that the defendant is innocent, whereas the alternative hypothesis, H1, asserts that the defendant is [guilty](/wiki/Guilt_(law) "Guilt (law)"). The [indictment](/wiki/Indictment "Indictment") comes because of suspicion of the guilt. The H0 (the [status quo](/wiki/Status_quo "Status quo")) stands in opposition to H1 and is maintained unless H1 is supported by evidence "beyond a reasonable doubt". However, "failure to reject H0" in this case does not imply innocence, but merely that the evidence was insufficient to convict. So the jury does not necessarily *accept* H0 but *fails to reject* H0. While one can not "prove" a null hypothesis, one can test how close it is to being true with a [power test](/wiki/Statistical_power "Statistical power"), which tests for [type II errors](/wiki/Type_II_error "Type II error").

##### Error

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Error")]

Working from a [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis"), two broad categories of error are recognized:

* [Type I errors](/wiki/Type_I_and_type_II_errors#Type_I_error "Type I and type II errors") where the null hypothesis is falsely rejected, giving a "false positive".
* [Type II errors](/wiki/Type_I_and_type_II_errors#Type_II_error "Type I and type II errors") where the null hypothesis fails to be rejected and an actual difference between populations is missed, giving a "false negative".

[Standard deviation](/wiki/Standard_deviation "Standard deviation") refers to the extent to which individual observations in a sample differ from a central value, such as the sample or population mean, while [Standard error](/wiki/Standard_error_(statistics)#Standard_error_of_the_mean "Standard error (statistics)") refers to an estimate of difference between sample mean and population mean.

A [statistical error](/wiki/Errors_and_residuals_in_statistics#Introduction "Errors and residuals in statistics") is the amount by which an observation differs from its [expected value](/wiki/Expected_value "Expected value"). A [residual](/wiki/Errors_and_residuals_in_statistics#Introduction "Errors and residuals in statistics") is the amount an observation differs from the value the estimator of the expected value assumes on a given sample (also called prediction).

[Mean squared error](/wiki/Mean_squared_error "Mean squared error") is used for obtaining [efficient estimators](/wiki/Efficient_estimators "Efficient estimators"), a widely used class of estimators. [Root mean square error](/wiki/Root_mean_square_error "Root mean square error") is simply the square root of mean squared error.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Linear_least_squares%282%29.svg/250px-Linear_least_squares%282%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Linear_least_squares(2).svg)

A least squares fit: in red the points to be fitted, in blue the fitted line.

Many statistical methods seek to minimize the [residual sum of squares](/wiki/Residual_sum_of_squares "Residual sum of squares"), and these are called "[methods of least squares](/wiki/Least_squares "Least squares")" in contrast to [Least absolute deviations](/wiki/Least_absolute_deviations "Least absolute deviations"). The latter gives equal weight to small and big errors, while the former gives more weight to large errors. Residual sum of squares is also [differentiable](/wiki/Differentiable_function "Differentiable function"), which provides a handy property for doing [regression](/wiki/Regression_analysis "Regression analysis"). Least squares applied to [linear regression](/wiki/Linear_regression "Linear regression") is called [ordinary least squares](/wiki/Ordinary_least_squares "Ordinary least squares") method and least squares applied to [nonlinear regression](/wiki/Nonlinear_regression "Nonlinear regression") is called [non-linear least squares](/wiki/Non-linear_least_squares "Non-linear least squares"). Also in a linear regression model the non deterministic part of the model is called error term, disturbance or more simply noise. Both linear regression and non-linear regression are addressed in [polynomial least squares](/wiki/Polynomial_least_squares "Polynomial least squares"), which also describes the variance in a prediction of the dependent variable (y axis) as a function of the independent variable (x axis) and the deviations (errors, noise, disturbances) from the estimated (fitted) curve.

Measurement processes that generate statistical data are also subject to error. Many of these errors are classified as [random](/wiki/Random_error "Random error") (noise) or [systematic](/wiki/Systematic_error "Systematic error") ([bias](/wiki/Bias "Bias")), but other types of errors (e.g., blunder, such as when an analyst reports incorrect units) can also be important. The presence of [missing data](/wiki/Missing_data "Missing data") or [censoring](/wiki/Censoring_(statistics) "Censoring (statistics)") may result in [biased estimates](/wiki/Bias_(statistics) "Bias (statistics)") and specific techniques have been developed to address these problems.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

##### Interval estimation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Interval estimation")]

Main article: [Interval estimation](/wiki/Interval_estimation "Interval estimation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/NYW-confidence-interval.svg/250px-NYW-confidence-interval.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NYW-confidence-interval.svg)

[Confidence intervals](/wiki/Confidence_intervals "Confidence intervals"): the red line is true value for the mean in this example, the blue lines are random confidence intervals for 100 realizations.

Most studies only sample part of a population, so results do not fully represent the whole population. Any estimates obtained from the sample only approximate the population value. [Confidence intervals](/wiki/Confidence_intervals "Confidence intervals") allow statisticians to express how closely the sample estimate matches the true value in the whole population. Often they are expressed as 95% confidence intervals. Formally, a 95% confidence interval for a value is a range where, if the sampling and analysis were repeated under the same conditions (yielding a different dataset), the interval would include the true (population) value in 95% of all possible cases. This does *not* imply that the probability that the true value is in the confidence interval is 95%. From the [frequentist](/wiki/Frequentist_inference "Frequentist inference") perspective, such a claim does not even make sense, as the true value is not a [random variable](/wiki/Random_variable "Random variable"). Either the true value is or is not within the given interval. However, it is true that, before any data are sampled and given a plan for how to construct the confidence interval, the probability is 95% that the yet-to-be-calculated interval will cover the true value: at this point, the limits of the interval are yet-to-be-observed [random variables](/wiki/Random_variable "Random variable"). One approach that does yield an interval that can be interpreted as having a given probability of containing the true value is to use a [credible interval](/wiki/Credible_interval "Credible interval") from [Bayesian statistics](/wiki/Bayesian_statistics "Bayesian statistics"): this approach depends on a different way of [interpreting what is meant by "probability"](/wiki/Probability_interpretations "Probability interpretations"), that is as a [Bayesian probability](/wiki/Bayesian_probability "Bayesian probability").

In principle, confidence intervals can be symmetrical or asymmetrical. An interval can be asymmetrical because it works as a lower or upper bound for a parameter (left-sided interval or right sided interval), but it can also be asymmetrical if a two-sided interval is built violating symmetry around the estimate. Sometimes the bounds of a confidence interval are reached asymptotically, and these are used to approximate the true bounds.

##### Significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Significance")]

Main article: [Statistical significance](/wiki/Statistical_significance "Statistical significance")

Statistics rarely give a simple Yes/No type answer to the question under analysis. Interpretation often comes down to the level of statistical significance applied to the numbers and often refers to the probability of a value accurately rejecting the null hypothesis (sometimes referred to as the [p-value](/wiki/P-value "P-value")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/P-value_in_statistical_significance_testing.svg/500px-P-value_in_statistical_significance_testing.svg.png)](/wiki/File:P-value_in_statistical_significance_testing.svg)

In this graph the black line is probability distribution for the [test statistic](/wiki/Test_statistic "Test statistic"), the [critical region](/wiki/Critical_region#Definition_of_terms "Critical region") is the set of values to the right of the observed data point (observed value of the test statistic) and the [p-value](/wiki/P-value "P-value") is represented by the green area.

The standard approach[[31]](#cite_note-Piazza-31) is to test a null hypothesis against an alternative hypothesis. A [critical region](/wiki/Critical_region#Definition_of_terms "Critical region") is the set of values of the estimator that leads to refuting the null hypothesis. The probability of type I error is therefore the probability that the estimator belongs to the critical region given that null hypothesis is true ([statistical significance](/wiki/Statistical_significance "Statistical significance")) and the probability of type II error is the probability that the estimator does not belong to the critical region given that the alternative hypothesis is true. The [statistical power](/wiki/Statistical_power "Statistical power") of a test is the probability that it correctly rejects the null hypothesis when the null hypothesis is false.

Referring to statistical significance does not necessarily mean that the overall result is significant in real-world terms. For example, in a large study of a drug it may be shown that the drug has a statistically significant but very small beneficial effect, such that it is unlikely to help the patient noticeably.

Although in principle the acceptable level of statistical significance may be subject to debate, the [significance level](/wiki/Significance_level "Significance level") is the largest p-value that allows the test to reject the null hypothesis. This test is logically equivalent to saying that the p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing a result at least as extreme as the [test statistic](/wiki/Test_statistic "Test statistic"). Therefore, the smaller the significance level, the lower the probability of committing type I error.

Some problems are usually associated with this framework (See [criticism of hypothesis testing](/wiki/Statistical_hypothesis_testing#Criticism "Statistical hypothesis testing")):

* A difference that is highly statistically significant can still be of no practical significance, but it is possible to properly formulate tests to account for this. One response involves going beyond reporting only the [significance level](/wiki/Significance_level "Significance level") to include the [*p*-value](/wiki/P-value "P-value") when reporting whether a hypothesis is rejected or accepted. The p-value, however, does not indicate the [size](/wiki/Effect_size "Effect size") or importance of the observed effect and can also seem to exaggerate the importance of minor differences in large studies. A better and increasingly common approach is to report [confidence intervals](/wiki/Confidence_interval "Confidence interval"). Although these are produced from the same calculations as those of hypothesis tests or *p*-values, they describe both the size of the effect and the uncertainty surrounding it.
* Fallacy of the transposed conditional, aka [prosecutor's fallacy](/wiki/Prosecutor%27s_fallacy "Prosecutor's fallacy"): criticisms arise because the hypothesis testing approach forces one hypothesis (the [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis")) to be favored, since what is being evaluated is the probability of the observed result given the null hypothesis and not probability of the null hypothesis given the observed result. An alternative to this approach is offered by [Bayesian inference](/wiki/Bayesian_inference "Bayesian inference"), although it requires establishing a [prior probability](/wiki/Prior_probability "Prior probability").[[35]](#cite_note-Ioannidis2005-35)
* Rejecting the null hypothesis does not automatically prove the alternative hypothesis.
* As everything in [inferential statistics](/wiki/Inferential_statistics "Inferential statistics") it relies on sample size, and therefore under [fat tails](/wiki/Fat_tails "Fat tails") p-values may be seriously mis-computed.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Bayesian statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bayesian statistics")]

Main article: [Bayesian statistics](/wiki/Bayesian_statistics "Bayesian statistics")

An alternative paradigm to the popular [frequentist](/wiki/Frequentist_inference "Frequentist inference") paradigm is to use [Bayes' theorem](/wiki/Bayes%27_theorem "Bayes' theorem") to update the [prior probability](/wiki/Prior_probability "Prior probability") of the hypotheses in consideration based on the [relative likelihood](/wiki/Likelihood_function "Likelihood function") of the evidence gathered to obtain a [posterior probability](/wiki/Posterior_probability "Posterior probability"). Bayesian methods have been aided by the increase in available computing power to compute the [posterior probability](/wiki/Posterior_probability "Posterior probability") using numerical approximation techniques like [Markov Chain Monte Carlo](/wiki/Markov_Chain_Monte_Carlo "Markov Chain Monte Carlo").

For statistically modelling purposes, Bayesian models tend to be [hierarchical](/wiki/Bayesian_hierarchical_modeling "Bayesian hierarchical modeling"), for example, one could model each [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel as having video views distributed as a normal distribution with channel dependent mean and variance ![{\displaystyle {\mathcal {N}}(\mu _{i},\sigma _{i})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f787659b2423bdac1c28db186f537bd3a75899ab), while modeling the channel means as themselves coming from a normal distribution representing the distribution of average video view counts per channel, and the variances as coming from another distribution.

The concept of using [likelihood ratio](/wiki/Likelihood_function#Likelihood_ratio "Likelihood function") can also be prominently seen in [medical diagnostic testing](/wiki/Likelihood_ratios_in_diagnostic_testing "Likelihood ratios in diagnostic testing").

### Exploratory data analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Exploratory data analysis")]

Main article: [Exploratory data analysis](/wiki/Exploratory_data_analysis "Exploratory data analysis")

**Exploratory data analysis** (**EDA**) is an approach to [analyzing](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis") [data sets](/wiki/Data_set "Data set") to summarize their main characteristics, often with visual methods. A [statistical model](/wiki/Statistical_model "Statistical model") can be used or not, but primarily EDA is for seeing what the data can tell us beyond the formal modeling or hypothesis testing task.

### Mathematical statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Mathematical statistics")]

Main article: [Mathematical statistics](/wiki/Mathematical_statistics "Mathematical statistics")

Mathematical statistics is the application of mathematics to statistics. Mathematical techniques used for this include [mathematical analysis](/wiki/Mathematical_analysis "Mathematical analysis"), [linear algebra](/wiki/Linear_algebra "Linear algebra"), [stochastic analysis](/wiki/Stochastic_analysis "Stochastic analysis"), [differential equations](/wiki/Differential_equations "Differential equations"), and [measure-theoretic probability theory](/wiki/Measure-theoretic_probability_theory "Measure-theoretic probability theory").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) All statistical analyses make use of at least some mathematics, and mathematical statistics can therefore be regarded as a fundamental component of general statistics.[[8]](#cite_note-Moses-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of statistics](/wiki/History_of_statistics "History of statistics") and [Founders of statistics](/wiki/Founders_of_statistics "Founders of statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Jakob_Bernoulli.jpg/250px-Jakob_Bernoulli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakob_Bernoulli.jpg)

Bernoulli's *[Ars Conjectandi](/wiki/Ars_Conjectandi "Ars Conjectandi")* was the first work that dealt with [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory") as currently understood.

Formal discussions on inference date back to the [mathematicians](/wiki/Mathematics_in_medieval_Islam "Mathematics in medieval Islam") and [cryptographers](/wiki/Cryptographers "Cryptographers") of the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") between the 8th and 13th centuries. [Al-Khalil](/wiki/Al-Khalil_ibn_Ahmad_al-Farahidi "Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi") (717–786) wrote the *Book of Cryptographic Messages*, which contains one of the first uses of [permutations](/wiki/Permutation "Permutation") and [combinations](/wiki/Combination "Combination"), to list all possible Arabic words with and without vowels.[[36]](#cite_note-LB-36) [Al-Kindi](/wiki/Al-Kindi "Al-Kindi")'s *Manuscript on Deciphering Cryptographic Messages* gave a detailed description of how to use [frequency analysis](/wiki/Frequency_analysis "Frequency analysis") to decipher [encrypted](/wiki/Encrypted "Encrypted") messages, providing an early example of [statistical inference](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference") for [decoding](/wiki/Code "Code"). [Ibn Adlan](/wiki/Ibn_Adlan "Ibn Adlan") (1187–1268) later made an important contribution on the use of [sample size](/wiki/Sample_size "Sample size") in frequency analysis.[[36]](#cite_note-LB-36)

Although the term *statistic* was introduced by the Italian scholar [Girolamo Ghilini](/wiki/Girolamo_Ghilini "Girolamo Ghilini") in 1589 with reference to a collection of facts and information about a state, it was the German [Gottfried Achenwall](/wiki/Gottfried_Achenwall "Gottfried Achenwall") in 1749 who started using the term as a collection of quantitative information, in the modern use for this science.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) The earliest writing containing statistics in Europe dates back to 1663, with the publication of *[Natural and Political Observations upon the Bills of Mortality](/wiki/Natural_and_Political_Observations_upon_the_Bills_of_Mortality "Natural and Political Observations upon the Bills of Mortality")* by [John Graunt](/wiki/John_Graunt "John Graunt").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Early applications of statistical thinking revolved around the needs of states to base policy on demographic and economic data, hence its [*stat-* etymology](/wiki/History_of_statistics#Etymology "History of statistics"). The scope of the discipline of statistics broadened in the early 19th century to include the collection and analysis of data in general. Today, statistics is widely employed in government, business, and natural and social sciences.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss_1840_by_Jensen.jpg/250px-Carl_Friedrich_Gauss_1840_by_Jensen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Friedrich_Gauss_1840_by_Jensen.jpg)

[Carl Friedrich Gauss](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss "Carl Friedrich Gauss") made major contributions to probabilistic methods leading to statistics.

The mathematical foundations of statistics developed from discussions concerning [games of chance](/wiki/Game_of_chance "Game of chance") among mathematicians such as [Gerolamo Cardano](/wiki/Gerolamo_Cardano "Gerolamo Cardano"), [Blaise Pascal](/wiki/Blaise_Pascal "Blaise Pascal"), [Pierre de Fermat](/wiki/Pierre_de_Fermat "Pierre de Fermat"), and [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens"). Although the idea of probability was already examined in ancient and medieval law and philosophy (such as the work of [Juan Caramuel](/wiki/Juan_Caramuel "Juan Caramuel")), [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory") as a mathematical discipline only took shape at the very end of the 17th century, particularly in [Jacob Bernoulli](/wiki/Jacob_Bernoulli "Jacob Bernoulli")'s posthumous work *[Ars Conjectandi](/wiki/Ars_Conjectandi "Ars Conjectandi")*.[[40]](#cite_note-40) This was the first book where the realm of games of chance and the realm of the probable (which concerned opinion, evidence, and argument) were combined and submitted to mathematical analysis.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The [method of least squares](/wiki/Method_of_least_squares "Method of least squares") was first described by [Adrien-Marie Legendre](/wiki/Adrien-Marie_Legendre "Adrien-Marie Legendre") in 1805, though [Carl Friedrich Gauss](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss "Carl Friedrich Gauss") presumably made use of it a decade earlier in 1795.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Karl_Pearson%2C_1910.jpg/250px-Karl_Pearson%2C_1910.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Pearson,_1910.jpg)

[Karl Pearson](/wiki/Karl_Pearson "Karl Pearson"), a founder of mathematical statistics

In the 1830s-1850s, "statistical offices" and national "statistical societies" were founded in Europe and America, and in the mid-19th century, the idea arose of "organized contacts between the statisticians of different countries although informal contacts occurred earlier".[[43]](#cite_note-43) In those days, the name "statistics" referred mainly to "matters of state", and British statisticians were often called "statists".[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Belgian scientist [Adolphe Quetelet](/wiki/Adolphe_Quetelet "Adolphe Quetelet") (1796–1874) introduced the notion of the "average man" (*l'homme moyen*) as a means of understanding complex social phenomena such as [crime rates](/wiki/Crime_rates "Crime rates"), [marriage rates](/wiki/Marriage_rates "Marriage rates"), and [suicide rates](/wiki/Suicide_rates "Suicide rates").[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 1853 Quetelet organised in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") the [First International Statistical Congress](/wiki/First_International_Statistical_Congress "First International Statistical Congress") in order to unify measurement in statistical research.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The modern field of statistics emerged in the late 19th and early 20th century in three stages.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The first wave, at the turn of the century, was led by the work of [Francis Galton](/wiki/Francis_Galton "Francis Galton") and [Karl Pearson](/wiki/Karl_Pearson "Karl Pearson"), who transformed statistics into a rigorous mathematical discipline used for analysis, not just in science, but in industry and politics as well. Galton's contributions included introducing the concepts of [standard deviation](/wiki/Standard_deviation "Standard deviation"), [correlation](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation"), [regression analysis](/wiki/Regression_analysis "Regression analysis") and the application of these methods to the study of the variety of human characteristics—height, weight and eyelash length among others.[[48]](#cite_note-Galton1877-48) Pearson developed the [Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient](/wiki/Pearson_product-moment_correlation_coefficient "Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient"), defined as a product-moment,[[49]](#cite_note-49) the [method of moments](/wiki/Method_of_moments_(statistics) "Method of moments (statistics)") for the fitting of distributions to samples and the [Pearson distribution](/wiki/Pearson_distribution "Pearson distribution"), among many other things.[[50]](#cite_note-Pearson,_On_the_criterion-50) Galton and Pearson founded *[Biometrika](/wiki/Biometrika "Biometrika")* as the first journal of mathematical statistics and [biostatistics](/wiki/Biostatistics "Biostatistics") (then called *biometry*), and the latter founded the world's first university statistics department at [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The second wave of the 1910s and 20s was initiated by [William Sealy Gosset](/wiki/William_Sealy_Gosset "William Sealy Gosset"), and reached its culmination in the insights of [Ronald Fisher](/wiki/Ronald_Fisher "Ronald Fisher"), who wrote the textbooks that were to define the academic discipline in universities around the world. Fisher's most important publications were his 1918 seminal paper *[The Correlation between Relatives on the Supposition of Mendelian Inheritance](/wiki/The_Correlation_between_Relatives_on_the_Supposition_of_Mendelian_Inheritance "The Correlation between Relatives on the Supposition of Mendelian Inheritance")* (which was the first to use the statistical term, [variance](/wiki/Variance "Variance")), his classic 1925 work *[Statistical Methods for Research Workers](/wiki/Statistical_Methods_for_Research_Workers "Statistical Methods for Research Workers")* and his 1935 *[The Design of Experiments](/wiki/The_Design_of_Experiments "The Design of Experiments")*,[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) where he developed rigorous [design of experiments](/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments") models. He originated the concepts of [sufficiency](/wiki/Sufficiency_(statistics) "Sufficiency (statistics)"), [ancillary statistics](/wiki/Ancillary_statistic "Ancillary statistic"), [Fisher's linear discriminator](/wiki/Linear_discriminant_analysis "Linear discriminant analysis") and [Fisher information](/wiki/Fisher_information "Fisher information").[[55]](#cite_note-55) He also coined the term [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis") during the [Lady tasting tea](/wiki/Lady_tasting_tea "Lady tasting tea") experiment, which "is never proved or established, but is possibly disproved, in the course of experimentation".[[56]](#cite_note-oed-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) In his 1930 book *[The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection](/wiki/The_Genetical_Theory_of_Natural_Selection "The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection")*, he applied statistics to various [biological](/wiki/Biology "Biology") concepts such as [Fisher's principle](/wiki/Fisher%27s_principle "Fisher's principle")[[58]](#cite_note-Edwards98-58) (which [A. W. F. Edwards](/wiki/A._W._F._Edwards "A. W. F. Edwards") called "probably the most celebrated argument in [evolutionary biology](/wiki/Evolutionary_biology "Evolutionary biology")") and [Fisherian runaway](/wiki/Fisherian_runaway "Fisherian runaway"),[[59]](#cite_note-fisher15-59)[[60]](#cite_note-fisher30-60)[[61]](#cite_note-pers00-61)[[62]](#cite_note-ander94-62)[[63]](#cite_note-ander06-63)[[64]](#cite_note-gayon10-64) a concept in [sexual selection](/wiki/Sexual_selection "Sexual selection") about a positive feedback runaway effect found in [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution").

The final wave, which mainly saw the refinement and expansion of earlier developments, emerged from the collaborative work between [Egon Pearson](/wiki/Egon_Pearson "Egon Pearson") and [Jerzy Neyman](/wiki/Jerzy_Neyman "Jerzy Neyman") in the 1930s. They introduced the concepts of "[Type II](/wiki/Type_I_and_type_II_errors "Type I and type II errors")" error, [power of a test](/wiki/Power_of_a_test "Power of a test") and [confidence intervals](/wiki/Confidence_interval "Confidence interval"). Jerzy Neyman in 1934 showed that stratified random sampling was in general a better method of estimation than purposive (quota) sampling.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Among the early attempts to measure national economic activity were those of [William Petty](/wiki/William_Petty "William Petty") in the 17th century. In the 20th century the uniform [System of National Accounts](/wiki/System_of_National_Accounts "System of National Accounts") was developed.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Today, statistical methods are applied in all fields that involve decision making, for making accurate inferences from a collated body of data and for making decisions in the face of uncertainty based on statistical methodology. The use of modern [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer") has expedited large-scale statistical computations and has also made possible new methods that are impractical to perform manually. Statistics continues to be an area of active research, for example on the problem of how to analyze [big data](/wiki/Big_data "Big data").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Applied statistics, theoretical statistics and mathematical statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Applied statistics, theoretical statistics and mathematical statistics")]

*Applied statistics,* sometimes referred to as *Statistical science,*[[68]](#cite_note-68) comprises descriptive statistics and the application of inferential statistics.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) *Theoretical statistics* concerns the logical arguments underlying justification of approaches to [statistical inference](/wiki/Statistical_inference "Statistical inference"), as well as encompassing *mathematical statistics*. Mathematical statistics includes not only the manipulation of [probability distributions](/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution") necessary for deriving results related to methods of estimation and inference, but also various aspects of [computational statistics](/wiki/Computational_statistics "Computational statistics") and the [design of experiments](/wiki/Design_of_experiments "Design of experiments").

[Statistical consultants](/wiki/Statistical_consultant "Statistical consultant") can help organizations and companies that do not have in-house expertise relevant to their particular questions.

### Machine learning and data mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Machine learning and data mining")]

[Machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") models are statistical and probabilistic models that capture patterns in the data through use of computational algorithms.

### Statistics in academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Statistics in academia")]

Statistics is applicable to a wide variety of [academic disciplines](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline"), including [natural](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science") and [social sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science"), government, and business. Business statistics applies statistical methods in [econometrics](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics"), [auditing](/wiki/Auditing "Auditing") and production and operations, including services improvement and marketing research.[[71]](#cite_note-71) A study of two journals in tropical biology found that the 12 most frequent statistical tests are: [analysis of variance](/wiki/Analysis_of_variance "Analysis of variance") (ANOVA), [chi-squared test](/wiki/Chi-squared_test "Chi-squared test"), [Student's t-test](/wiki/Student%27s_t-test "Student's t-test"), [linear regression](/wiki/Linear_regression "Linear regression"), [Pearson's correlation coefficient](/wiki/Pearson%27s_correlation_coefficient "Pearson's correlation coefficient"), [Mann-Whitney U test](/wiki/Mann-Whitney_U_test "Mann-Whitney U test"), [Kruskal-Wallis test](/wiki/Kruskal-Wallis_test "Kruskal-Wallis test"), [Shannon's diversity index](/wiki/Diversity_index#Shannon_index "Diversity index"), [Tukey's range test](/wiki/Tukey%27s_range_test "Tukey's range test"), [cluster analysis](/wiki/Cluster_analysis "Cluster analysis"), [Spearman's rank correlation coefficient](/wiki/Spearman%27s_rank_correlation_coefficient "Spearman's rank correlation coefficient") and [principal component analysis](/wiki/Principal_component_analysis "Principal component analysis").[[72]](#cite_note-:0-72)

A typical statistics course covers descriptive statistics, probability, binomial and [normal distributions](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution"), test of hypotheses and confidence intervals, [linear regression](/wiki/Linear_regression "Linear regression"), and correlation.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Modern fundamental statistical courses for undergraduate students focus on correct test selection, results interpretation, and use of free statistics software.[[72]](#cite_note-:0-72)

### Statistical computing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Statistical computing")]

Main article: [Computational statistics](/wiki/Computational_statistics "Computational statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Gretl_screenshot.png/330px-Gretl_screenshot.png)](/wiki/File:Gretl_screenshot.png)

[gretl](/wiki/Gretl "Gretl"), an example of an [open source statistical package](/wiki/List_of_open_source_statistical_packages "List of open source statistical packages")

The rapid and sustained increases in computing power starting from the second half of the 20th century have had a substantial impact on the practice of statistical science. Early statistical models were almost always from the class of [linear models](/wiki/Linear_model "Linear model"), but powerful computers, coupled with suitable numerical [algorithms](/wiki/Algorithms "Algorithms"), caused an increased interest in [nonlinear models](/wiki/Nonlinear_regression "Nonlinear regression") (such as [neural networks](/wiki/Artificial_neural_network "Artificial neural network")) as well as the creation of new types, such as [generalized linear models](/wiki/Generalized_linear_model "Generalized linear model") and [multilevel models](/wiki/Multilevel_model "Multilevel model").

Increased computing power has also led to the growing popularity of computationally intensive methods based on [resampling](/wiki/Resampling_(statistics) "Resampling (statistics)"), such as [permutation tests](/wiki/Permutation_test "Permutation test") and the [bootstrap](/wiki/Bootstrapping_(statistics) "Bootstrapping (statistics)"), while techniques such as [Gibbs sampling](/wiki/Gibbs_sampling "Gibbs sampling") have made use of [Bayesian models](/wiki/Bayesian_model "Bayesian model") more feasible. The computer revolution has implications for the future of statistics with a new emphasis on "experimental" and "empirical" statistics. A large number of both general and special purpose [statistical software](/wiki/List_of_statistical_packages "List of statistical packages") are now available. Examples of available software capable of complex statistical computation include programs such as [Mathematica](/wiki/Mathematica "Mathematica"), [SAS](/wiki/SAS_(software) "SAS (software)"), [SPSS](/wiki/SPSS "SPSS"), and [R](/wiki/R_(programming_language) "R (programming language)").

### Business statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Business statistics")]

See also: [Business mathematics § University level](/wiki/Business_mathematics#University_level "Business mathematics")

In business, "statistics" is a widely used [management-](/wiki/Management#Nature_of_work "Management") and [decision support](/wiki/Decision_support "Decision support") tool. It is particularly applied in [financial management](/wiki/Financial_management "Financial management"), [marketing management](/wiki/Marketing_management "Marketing management"), and [production](/wiki/Manufacturing_process_management "Manufacturing process management"), [services](/wiki/Operations_management_for_services "Operations management for services") and [operations management](/wiki/Operations_management "Operations management").[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) Statistics is also heavily used in [management accounting](/wiki/Management_accounting "Management accounting") and [auditing](/wiki/Auditing "Auditing"). The discipline of [Management Science](/wiki/Management_Science "Management Science") formalizes the use of statistics, and other mathematics, in business. ([Econometrics](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics") is the application of statistical methods to [economic data](/wiki/Economic_data "Economic data") in order to give empirical content to [economic relationships](/wiki/Economic_theory "Economic theory").)

A typical "Business Statistics" course is intended for [business majors](/wiki/Business_education#Undergraduate_education "Business education"), and covers[[76]](#cite_note-76) [descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics") ([collection](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection"), description, analysis, and summary of data), probability (typically the [binomial](/wiki/Binomial_distribution "Binomial distribution") and [normal distributions](/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution")), test of hypotheses and confidence intervals, [linear regression](/wiki/Linear_regression "Linear regression"), and correlation; (follow-on) courses may include [forecasting](/wiki/Forecasting "Forecasting"), [time series](/wiki/Time_series "Time series"), [decision trees](/wiki/Decision_trees "Decision trees"), [multiple linear regression](/wiki/Multiple_linear_regression "Multiple linear regression"), and other topics from [business analytics](/wiki/Business_analytics "Business analytics") more generally. [Professional certification programs](/wiki/Professional_certification_in_financial_services "Professional certification in financial services"), such as the [CFA](/wiki/Chartered_Financial_Analyst "Chartered Financial Analyst"), often include topics in statistics.

## Specialized disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Specialized disciplines")]

Main article: [List of fields of application of statistics](/wiki/List_of_fields_of_application_of_statistics "List of fields of application of statistics")

Statistical techniques are used in a wide range of types of scientific and social research, including: [biostatistics](/wiki/Biostatistics "Biostatistics"), [computational biology](/wiki/Computational_biology "Computational biology"), [computational sociology](/wiki/Computational_sociology "Computational sociology"), [network biology](/wiki/Network_biology "Network biology"), [social science](/wiki/Social_science "Social science"), [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") and [social research](/wiki/Social_research "Social research"). Some fields of inquiry use applied statistics so extensively that they have [specialized terminology](/wiki/Specialized_terminology "Specialized terminology"). These disciplines include:

* [Actuarial science](/wiki/Actuarial_science "Actuarial science") (assesses risk in the insurance and finance industries)
* Applied information economics
* [Astrostatistics](/wiki/Astrostatistics "Astrostatistics") (statistical evaluation of astronomical data)
* [Biostatistics](/wiki/Biostatistics "Biostatistics")
* [Chemometrics](/wiki/Chemometrics "Chemometrics") (for analysis of data from [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"))
* [Data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining") (applying statistics and [pattern recognition](/wiki/Pattern_recognition "Pattern recognition") to discover knowledge from data)
* [Data science](/wiki/Data_science "Data science")
* [Demography](/wiki/Demography "Demography") (statistical study of populations)
* [Econometrics](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics") (statistical analysis of economic data)
* [Energy statistics](/wiki/Statistical_study_of_energy_data "Statistical study of energy data")
* [Engineering statistics](/wiki/Engineering_statistics "Engineering statistics")
* [Epidemiology](/wiki/Epidemiology "Epidemiology") (statistical analysis of disease)
* [Geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography") and [geographic information systems](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system"), specifically in [spatial analysis](/wiki/Spatial_analysis "Spatial analysis")
* [Image processing](/wiki/Image_processing "Image processing")
* [Jurimetrics](/wiki/Jurimetrics "Jurimetrics") ([law](/wiki/Law "Law"))
* [Medical statistics](/wiki/Medical_statistics "Medical statistics")
* [Political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science")
* [Psychological statistics](/wiki/Psychological_statistics "Psychological statistics")
* [Reliability engineering](/wiki/Reliability_engineering "Reliability engineering")
* [Social statistics](/wiki/Social_statistics "Social statistics")
* [Statistical mechanics](/wiki/Statistical_mechanics "Statistical mechanics")

In addition, there are particular types of statistical analysis that have also developed their own specialised terminology and methodology:

* [Bootstrap](/wiki/Bootstrapping_(statistics) "Bootstrapping (statistics)") / [jackknife resampling](/wiki/Jackknife_resampling "Jackknife resampling")
* [Multivariate statistics](/wiki/Multivariate_statistics "Multivariate statistics")
* [Statistical classification](/wiki/Statistical_classification "Statistical classification")
* [Structured data analysis](/wiki/Structured_data_analysis_(statistics) "Structured data analysis (statistics)")
* [Structural equation modelling](/wiki/Structural_equation_modelling "Structural equation modelling")
* [Survey methodology](/wiki/Survey_methodology "Survey methodology")
* [Survival analysis](/wiki/Survival_analysis "Survival analysis")
* Statistics in various sports, particularly [baseball](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics") – known as [sabermetrics](/wiki/Sabermetrics "Sabermetrics") – and [cricket](/wiki/Cricket_statistics "Cricket statistics")

Statistics form a key basis tool in business and manufacturing as well. It is used to understand measurement systems variability, control processes (as in [statistical process control](/wiki/Statistical_process_control "Statistical process control") or SPC), for summarizing data, and to make data-driven decisions.

## Misuse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Misuse")]

Main article: [Misuse of statistics](/wiki/Misuse_of_statistics "Misuse of statistics")

[Misuse of statistics](/wiki/Misuse_of_statistics "Misuse of statistics") can produce subtle but serious errors in description and interpretation—subtle in the sense that even experienced professionals make such errors, and serious in the sense that they can lead to devastating decision errors. For instance, social policy, medical practice, and the reliability of structures like bridges all rely on the proper use of statistics.

Even when statistical techniques are correctly applied, the results can be difficult to interpret for those lacking expertise. The [statistical significance](/wiki/Statistical_significance "Statistical significance") of a trend in the data—which measures the extent to which a trend could be caused by random variation in the sample—may or may not agree with an intuitive sense of its significance. The set of basic statistical skills (and skepticism) that people need to deal with information in their everyday lives properly is referred to as [statistical literacy](/wiki/Statistical_literacy "Statistical literacy").

There is a general perception that statistical knowledge is all-too-frequently intentionally [misused](/wiki/Misuse_of_statistics "Misuse of statistics") by finding ways to interpret only the data that are favorable to the presenter.[[77]](#cite_note-Huff-77) A mistrust and misunderstanding of statistics is associated with the quotation, "[There are three kinds of lies: lies, damned lies, and statistics](/wiki/Lies,_damned_lies,_and_statistics "Lies, damned lies, and statistics")". Misuse of statistics can be both inadvertent and intentional, and the book *[How to Lie with Statistics](/wiki/How_to_Lie_with_Statistics "How to Lie with Statistics")*,[[77]](#cite_note-Huff-77) by [Darrell Huff](/wiki/Darrell_Huff "Darrell Huff"), outlines a range of considerations. In an attempt to shed light on the use and misuse of statistics, reviews of statistical techniques used in particular fields are conducted (e.g. Warne, Lazo, Ramos, and Ritter (2012)).[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Ways to avoid misuse of statistics include using proper diagrams and avoiding [bias](/wiki/Bias_(statistics) "Bias (statistics)").[[79]](#cite_note-Statistics_in_Archaeology-79) Misuse can occur when conclusions are [overgeneralized](/wiki/Hasty_generalization "Hasty generalization") and claimed to be representative of more than they really are, often by either deliberately or unconsciously overlooking sampling bias.[[80]](#cite_note-Misuse_of_Statistics-80) Bar graphs are arguably the easiest diagrams to use and understand, and they can be made either by hand or with simple computer programs.[[79]](#cite_note-Statistics_in_Archaeology-79) Most people do not look for bias or errors, so they are not noticed. Thus, people may often believe that something is true even if it is not well [represented](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)").[[80]](#cite_note-Misuse_of_Statistics-80) To make data gathered from statistics believable and accurate, the sample taken must be representative of the whole.[[81]](#cite_note-Modern_Elementary_Statistics-81) According to Huff, "The dependability of a sample can be destroyed by [bias]... allow yourself some degree of skepticism."[[82]](#cite_note-82)

To assist in the understanding of statistics Huff proposed a series of questions to be asked in each case:[[77]](#cite_note-Huff-77)

* Who says so? (Do they have an axe to grind?)
* How do they know? (Do they have the resources to know the facts?)
* What's missing? (Do they give us a complete picture?)
* Did someone change the subject? (Do they offer us the right answer to the wrong problem?)
* Does it make sense? (Is their conclusion logical and consistent with what we already know?)

### Misinterpretation: correlation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistics&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Misinterpretation: correlation")]

See also: [Correlation does not imply causation](/wiki/Correlation_does_not_imply_causation "Correlation does not imply causation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Simple_Confounding_Case.svg/250px-Simple_Confounding_Case.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Simple_Confounding_Case.svg)

The [confounding variable](/wiki/Confounding_variable "Confounding variable") problem: *X* and *Y* may be correlated, not because there is causal relationship between them, but because both depend on a third variable *Z*. *Z* is called a confounding factor.

The concept of [correlation](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation") is particularly noteworthy for the potential confusion it can cause. Statistical analysis of a [data set](/wiki/Data_set "Data set") often reveals that two variables (properties) of the population under consideration tend to vary together, as if they were connected. For example, a study of annual income that also looks at age of death, might find that poor people tend to have shorter lives than affluent people. The two variables are said to be correlated; however, they may or may not be the cause of one another. The correlation phenomena could be caused by a third, previously unconsidered phenomenon, called a lurking variable or [confounding variable](/wiki/Confounding_variable "Confounding variable"). For this reason, there is no way to immediately infer the existence of a causal relationship between the two variables.
